claims.claim: Noam Shazeer announced on June 18, 2026 that he is leaving Google to join OpenAI.
Shazeer's reported new OpenAI role is Lead for Architecture Research; the title was not stated in his own post.
Sam Altman publicly welcomed Shazeer, saying he is one of the people he most wanted to work with since the beginning of OpenAI and it 'only took 10 years'.
At departure Shazeer was VP of Engineering at Google and a co-lead of Gemini.

In August 2024 Google paid a reported ~$2.7 billion in a non-exclusive license to Character.AI's technology and brought Shazeer and Daniel De Freitas plus about 30 researchers back to Google DeepMind; Google took no equity stake.
Character.AI investors were bought out at roughly $88 per share, about 2.5x the 2023 Series A price, implying a buyout valuation near $2.5 billion (up from a ~$1 billion Series A valuation).
Shazeer, reportedly Character.AI's largest shareholder, is estimated to have personally netted roughly $750 million to $1 billion from the deal.
After the deal Character.AI abandoned building its own foundation models and pivoted to fine-tuning open-source models, reporting 20%+ jumps in time spent.
Character.AI's estimated valuation fell to about $1 billion in 2025 from a ~$2.5 billion peak in 2024.

The Megan Garcia wrongful-death suit against Character.AI and Google (filed Oct 2024) survived a dismissal motion in May 2025 and was settled in January 2026 on undisclosed terms; Character.AI barred under-18 open-ended chat effective Nov 25, 2025.
Shazeer co-authored Attention Is All You Need (2017, the Transformer, 8 authors), first-authored the sparsely-gated Mixture-of-Experts paper (2017), and sole-authored multi-query attention (2019).

Gemini's technical leadership was a named multi-person set (Shazeer alongside Jeff Dean and Oriol Vinyals, under Demis Hassabis and Koray Kavukcuoglu), built by hundreds of engineers across Google Brain and DeepMind.
All eight authors of Attention Is All You Need eventually left Google, yet Gemini 2.5 Pro debuted at #1 on LMArena in March 2025.
Microsoft paid Inflection ~$650M ($620M non-exclusive license + $30M legal-claims waiver) in March 2024 and hired Mustafa Suleyman and most staff, leaving Inflection independent.
Google DeepMind paid a reported ~$2.4 billion to license Windsurf's technology and hire its CEO and top staff in July 2025, taking no equity stake, after OpenAI's acquisition of Windsurf collapsed.
Google DeepMind hired 20+ Contextual AI researchers including CEO Douwe Kiela under a reported ~$80-100M licensing deal in May 2026, continuing the acquihire-avoids-merger-review pattern.
The DOJ told Google in May 2025 it is examining whether the Character.AI deal was structured to avoid formal merger scrutiny; the probe is early-stage with no enforcement action.
The FTC opened a 6(b) inquiry into AI partnerships in January 2024 and issued a staff report in January 2025 flagging risks to compute and engineering-talent access and switching costs.

A joint FTC/DOJ Request for Information in March 2026 explicitly named acquihires, reverse acquihires, and non-exclusive IP licenses as unreported transactions that can effectively eliminate a market participant.
SignalFire's State of Talent 2025 report put two-year retention at Anthropic 80%, DeepMind 78%, OpenAI 67%, Meta 64%, and found researchers leave DeepMind for Anthropic ~11x more than the reverse.
OpenAI paid about $1.5 million in average stock-based compensation per employee in 2025, the highest of any major tech startup on record.

Sam Altman alleged Meta offered $100M signing bonuses to poach OpenAI staff; Meta's CTO said the figure applied to a few senior roles and was not an upfront bonus, and an actual OpenAI-to-Meta hire denied receiving it.
Alphabet's FY2025 R&D expense was about $61.1 billion, and combined 2026 hyperscaler capex guidance (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) totals roughly $725 billion.
ChatGPT reached 800 million weekly active users as of October 2025.
GOOGL did not sell off on the June 18-19 2026 departures; monthly closes ran $338.00 (Jan) to $368.03 (Jun 2026), with the stock far above its March low.
Andrew Tulloch reportedly declined a Meta package of up to ~$1.5 billion over six years before later joining Meta; Altman framed talent retention as 'missionaries vs mercenaries'.
